title: HB 1181
measure_text_summary: Requires age verification for covered adult-content websites and provides a civil-enforcement framework.
status_detail: In force; upheld by SCOTUS on merits under intermediate scrutiny.
legal_obligations: Covered websites must verify age before access to qualifying content.
scope_of_application: Websites meeting statutory thresholds for harmful/adult content.
verification_requirements: Age verification, including statute-defined methods.
procedural_safeguards: Judicial review and constitutional litigation ongoing precedent context.
technical_standards_refs: No explicit single standard mandate in core statute text summary.

title: HB 1126 (Walker Montgomery Protecting Children Online Act)
measure_text_summary: Establishes minors-online protections including age registration and limits tied to digital service providers.
status_detail: Enjoined; preliminary injunction entered Jul 1, 2024 (same day as intended effective date) by Judge Halil Suleyman Ozerden (S.D. Miss.) in NetChoice v. Fitch; enforcement halted pending litigation.
last_court_action: Preliminary injunction entered by S.D. Miss.; SCOTUS denied application to vacate stay in related NetChoice emergency matter
legal_obligations: Requires covered digital service providers to implement age verification, parental consent, data minimization, and duty to prevent harmful content for minors.
scope_of_application: Digital service providers serving Mississippi users under 18, broadly covering social media, apps, and online services.
verification_requirements: Commercially reasonable age verification; parental consent for under-18s.
procedural_safeguards: Enforced through existing unfair/deceptive trade framework linkage.
technical_standards_refs: No single mandatory named standard identified in this seed summary.

title: SB 976 (Protecting Our Kids from Social Media Addiction Act)
measure_text_summary: Regulates addictive feeds and notification patterns for minors, with parental-consent and age-assurance-linked obligations.
status_detail: Partially Enjoined; time-of-day notification restrictions enjoined Dec 31 2024 by N.D. Cal. (Judge Davila) in NetChoice v. Bonta; age assurance requirements and addictive-feed restrictions remain in force; 9th Circuit appeal ongoing. Age verification element required by Dec 31, 2026.
last_court_action: Preliminary injunction on time-of-day restrictions entered by N.D. Cal. (Judge Davila); 9th Circuit appeal ongoing
legal_obligations: Operators must control addictive-feed access and notifications based on minor status/consent conditions.
scope_of_application: Operators of covered addictive internet-based services/applications serving California users under 18.
verification_requirements: Age assurance (background check basis); parental consent for minors to access addictive algorithmic feeds; full age verification required by Dec 31, 2026.
procedural_safeguards: Data-use limitations and severability clauses in statute.
technical_standards_refs: Regulations include age-assurance/parental-consent implementation details.

title: AB 1043 (Digital Age Assurance Act)
measure_text_summary: Creates operating-system/app-store to developer digital age-bracket signal obligations through API mechanisms.
status_detail: Approved 2025-10-13; operative date set by statute for 2027-01-01; under First Amendment challenge — CCIA v. California (N.D. Cal., filed Oct 16, 2025). In Force — Challenged.
last_court_action: CCIA v. California (N.D. Cal.) filed Oct 16, 2025; preliminary injunction motion status pending as of Apr 2026
legal_obligations: OS providers and developers must exchange age-bracket signals and apply related compliance duties.
scope_of_application: Covered application stores, OS providers, and developers under Title 1.81.9.
verification_requirements: Age bracket signals via real-time API categories (<13, 13-15, 16-17, 18+).
procedural_safeguards: Data minimization, anti-competitive-use restrictions, severability, civil penalties framework.
technical_standards_refs: Real-time API signal model required; no exclusive external standard mandated in seed extraction.

title: CS/CS/HB 3 (Online Protections for Minors)
measure_text_summary: Imposes age-related obligations on social media platforms, including account restrictions and parental-control pathways.
status_detail: Chapter 2024-42; effective 2025-01-01; active litigation context requires periodic posture checks.
last_court_action: Litigation posture active (latest docket action to be refreshed in litigation workflow)
enforcement_authority: Florida Department of Legal Affairs / Attorney General
legal_obligations: Platform duties tied to minors' account access and protections.
scope_of_application: Covered social media platforms under bill definitions.
verification_requirements: Age-related determination and parental consent mechanisms.
procedural_safeguards: Statutory remedies and enforcement through state legal framework.
technical_standards_refs: No single named standard required in this seed summary.

verification_requirements: Age-related assurance/signal requirements under enacted framework.
procedural_safeguards: Legislative process complete; codification-level enforcement details to confirm.

title: Kids Online Safety and Privacy Act (KOSPA) / Kids Online Safety Act (KOSA) (S.1409 / S.2073)
measure_text_summary: Federal proposal establishing a duty of care and minors-protection obligations for covered online platforms; Senate passed KOSPA 91-3 on Jul 30, 2024 but House did not advance before 118th Congress expired.
status_detail: Failed – Not Passed. Senate passed KOSPA 91-3 Jul 30, 2024; House Energy & Commerce Committee passed Sep 18, 2024; Speaker Johnson declined to advance before 118th Congress expired; reintroduced 119th Congress May 2025.
enforcement_authority: Federal Trade Commission and State Attorneys General (as proposed)
legal_obligations: Covered platforms must mitigate specified harms to minors and provide protective controls.
scope_of_application: Social media platforms, online video games, messaging apps, video streaming services used or likely to be used by minors under 17.
verification_requirements: Age verification/age estimation; duty of care obligations; parental tools; restricted design features; annual audits.
procedural_safeguards: Rulemaking, transparency reporting, and enforcement pathways in proposal text.
technical_standards_refs: No single mandatory external technical standard identified in current proposal text.

title: HB 1999 (Protecting Youth Online)
measure_text_summary: State proposal requiring online platforms to implement youth-protective design and age-related safeguards.
status_detail: Proposed measure; final enactment status pending in legislative process at last review.
legal_obligations: Would require platform safety-by-design controls and minors-specific risk mitigation.
scope_of_application: Covered online services and features addressed by proposal definitions.
verification_requirements: Age-estimation or age-assurance measures where required by bill provisions.
procedural_safeguards: Notice/compliance and enforcement procedures described in bill structure.
technical_standards_refs: No single mandatory external technical standard identified in proposal summary.

title: Title V, Telecommunications Act of 1996 – Communications Decency Act (CDA) (47 U.S.C. §§ 223(a)(1)(B), 223(d))
measure_text_summary: First federal attempt to regulate online indecency for minors; created criminal prohibitions on transmitting obscene or indecent messages online accessible to minors with AV-based affirmative defences. Struck down unanimously by SCOTUS on First Amendment grounds in 1997.
status_detail: Struck down – Reno v. ACLU, 521 U.S. 844 (1997); indecency provisions invalidated Jun 26, 1997 on First Amendment grounds; Section 230 (provider immunity) survived and remains in force.
last_court_action: Reno v. ACLU, 521 U.S. 844 (1997) – SCOTUS struck down indecency provisions
legal_obligations: Prohibited any person from transmitting obscene or indecent messages online accessible to minors under 18.
scope_of_application: Any person transmitting obscene or indecent messages online accessible to minors under 18.
verification_requirements: Criminal prohibition; affirmative defences included credit-card or adult-access-code age verification.
procedural_safeguards: Affirmative defence available to providers who restrict access using AV or adult-access codes.
technical_standards_refs: No exclusive technical standard mandated; credit-card or adult-access-code methods specified as affirmative defences.

title: Child Online Protection Act (COPA) (47 U.S.C. § 231)
measure_text_summary: Enacted as direct response to Reno v. ACLU; required commercial websites distributing material harmful to minors to restrict access via age verification. Permanently enjoined after 10 years of litigation; SCOTUS denied cert Jan 21, 2009.
status_detail: Permanently enjoined; never took effect; Supreme Court denied cert Jan 21, 2009 following Ashcroft v. ACLU (2002, 2004) and ACLU v. Mukasey, 534 F.3d 181 (3d Cir. 2008). Direct predecessor to state adult-content AV laws.
last_court_action: ACLU v. Mukasey, 534 F.3d 181 (3d Cir. 2008); cert. denied Jan 21, 2009
legal_obligations: Commercial websites distributing material harmful to minors on the WWW must restrict access via AV or adult-access code.
scope_of_application: Commercial websites distributing 'material harmful to minors' on the World Wide Web.
verification_requirements: Credit card or adult-access code for age verification; affirmative defences available.
procedural_safeguards: Affirmative defence for use of credit-card verification, adult-access codes, or other AV methods.
technical_standards_refs: Credit card or adult-access code specified; no exclusive external technical standard mandated.

title: Children's Online Privacy Protection Act (COPPA) (15 U.S.C. §§ 6501–6505 / 16 CFR Part 312)
measure_text_summary: FTC-enforced federal law prohibiting operators of websites or online services directed at children under 13, or with actual knowledge of under-13 users, from collecting personal information without verifiable parental consent.
status_detail: In force; COPPA Rule (16 CFR Part 312) issued Nov 1999, revised Jan 2013, further amended Jan 16, 2025 (eff. Apr 22, 2025); FTC issued Age Verification Policy Statement Feb 25, 2026.
legal_obligations: Operators must provide notice, obtain verifiable parental consent, and restrict data collection/use for children under 13.
scope_of_application: Operators of websites or online services directed at children under 13 OR with actual knowledge of collecting data from under-13 users.
verification_requirements: Verifiable Parental Consent (VPC) via FTC-approved methods: credit card, signed form, phone, email, video conference, government ID.
procedural_safeguards: FTC rulemaking; civil penalty enforcement; data security and retention requirements.
technical_standards_refs: FTC-approved VPC methods; 16 CFR Part 312; no single exclusive external technical standard.

title: COPPA Rule Amendment 2025 (16 CFR Part 312 – Final Rule, Jan 16, 2025)
measure_text_summary: First major COPPA Rule revision since 2013; enhances verifiable parental consent standards, prohibits certain consent methods (e.g., email plus), adds biometric identifiers to sensitive data categories, and introduces new data retention limits.
status_detail: In force effective Apr 22, 2025; signed by FTC Chair Khan Jan 16, 2025; FTC also issued separate Enforcement Policy Statement promoting age-verification technology Feb 25, 2026.
legal_obligations: Enhanced verifiable parental consent requirements; new sensitive data categories; tighter data retention limits; prohibition on certain prior-approved consent methods.
scope_of_application: Same as COPPA: operators of services directed to under-13s or with actual knowledge of under-13 users.
verification_requirements: Enhanced Verifiable Parental Consent standards; prohibits email-plus method; adds biometric identifiers to sensitive data.
procedural_safeguards: FTC rulemaking via NPRM (Dec 20, 2023); CRA process; civil penalty enforcement.
technical_standards_refs: 16 CFR Part 312 (amended); FTC-approved VPC methods; no single exclusive external technical standard.

title: AB 2273 – California Age-Appropriate Design Code Act (CAADCA)
measure_text_summary: First US Age-Appropriate Design Code; requires businesses offering online services likely to be accessed by children under 18 to conduct DPIAs and apply default privacy protections. Enjoined before taking effect; as of Mar 2026 most provisions remain enjoined.
status_detail: Partially Enjoined; D. Ct. prelim. injunction Sep 18, 2023; 9th Cir. partial vacatur Aug 16, 2024; 9th Cir. second ruling Mar 12, 2026 (5 of 6 provisions still enjoined); influential template for many state laws nationally.
last_court_action: 9th Cir. second ruling Mar 12, 2026 – 5 of 6 provisions still enjoined
legal_obligations: Businesses must conduct Data Protection Impact Assessments (DPIAs); apply default highest-privacy settings; prohibit dark patterns, profiling, geolocation, and addictive design for known children.
scope_of_application: Any business offering an online service, product, or feature 'likely to be accessed by children' under 18 that is subject to CA CCPA.
verification_requirements: Age estimation to 'reasonable level of certainty' (or apply protections to all users); DPIAs; default privacy settings.
procedural_safeguards: Severability; DPIA requirement; AG enforcement.
technical_standards_refs: No exclusive external technical standard mandated; modeled on UK Children's Code.

title: SB 152 / HB 311 – Utah Social Media Regulation Act (2023)
measure_text_summary: First US state social media age verification law; required government-ID age verification of all Utah users and parental consent for under-18s. Replaced by SB 194 in 2024.
status_detail: Repealed/Replaced by SB 194/HB 464 (Minor Protection in Social Media Act) signed Mar 7, 2024; preliminary injunction Sep 10, 2024 (Chief Judge Shelby) rendered moot after repeal.
last_court_action: NetChoice v. Reyes (D. Utah); preliminary injunction Sep 10, 2024 – moot after repeal
legal_obligations: Social media companies must perform government-ID age verification of all Utah users; parental consent for under-18s; no ID retention; time restrictions on minor access.
scope_of_application: Social media companies with 5 million+ worldwide users serving Utah users under 18.
verification_requirements: Government-ID based age verification of all Utah users; parental consent for under-18s; no retention of identifying information post-verification.
procedural_safeguards: Time restrictions (10:30pm–6:30am no minor access); parental account monitoring.
technical_standards_refs: No exclusive external technical standard mandated.

title: SB 194 / HB 464 – Utah Minor Protection in Social Media Act (2024)
measure_text_summary: Replacement for UT-SB152; enacted to cure constitutional defects identified in NetChoice lawsuit; also enjoined before its Oct 1, 2024 effective date by the same judge.
status_detail: Enjoined; preliminary injunction Sep 10, 2024 (Chief Judge Robert J. Shelby, D. Utah) before Oct 1, 2024 effective date; enforcement halted pending continued litigation.
last_court_action: NetChoice v. Brown (D. Utah); preliminary injunction Sep 10, 2024 (Chief Judge Robert J. Shelby)
legal_obligations: Age verification for all users; parental consent for minors; enhanced data protection requirements for minor accounts.
scope_of_application: Social media platforms serving Utah users under 18; replaces SB 152/HB 311.
verification_requirements: Age verification for all users; parental consent for minors to create/maintain accounts.
procedural_safeguards: Enhanced data protection requirements for minor accounts; civil enforcement.
technical_standards_refs: No exclusive external technical standard mandated.

title: SB 396 / Act 689 – Arkansas Social Media Safety Act (2023)
measure_text_summary: Required third-party age verification and parental consent for under-18s on social media platforms with $100M+ annual revenue serving Arkansas users. First social media law to receive a permanent injunction.
status_detail: Permanently enjoined; prelim. injunction Aug 31, 2023; permanent injunction Mar 31, 2025 (Judge Timothy L. Brooks, W.D. Ark.) – court held SB 396 unconstitutional under First Amendment; Arkansas appealing and enacted SB 611 (Apr 2025) as amendment.
last_court_action: NetChoice LLC v. Griffin, 2025 WL 978607 (W.D. Ark. Mar 31, 2025) – permanent injunction
legal_obligations: Social media companies must require third-party age verification and parental consent for under-18s; no retention of identifying information post-verification.
scope_of_application: Social media companies with $100M+ annual revenue serving Arkansas users under 18; excludes short-video and gaming platforms.
verification_requirements: Third-party age verification; parental consent for under-18s.
procedural_safeguards: No retention of identifying information post-verification; civil enforcement.
technical_standards_refs: No exclusive external technical standard mandated.

title: SB 3 / Public Act 23-56 – Act Concerning Online Privacy, Data and Safety Protections
measure_text_summary: Comprehensive children's online safety law amending the CT Data Privacy Act; prohibits addictive design features, targeted advertising, and geolocation collection for known minors under 18; requires DPAs and imposes duty of care.
status_detail: In force; passed unanimously (House 142-0, Senate 36-0); phased implementation: health data Jul 1, 2023; social media minors' obligations Jul 1, 2024; minors' data provisions Oct 1, 2024.
legal_obligations: Controllers must prohibit addictive design features, targeted advertising, geolocation collection without consent for known minors; duty of care; DPA requirement.
scope_of_application: Controllers offering online services, products, or features to consumers they know or willfully disregard are minors under 18; social media platforms with CT minor users.
verification_requirements: Based on actual knowledge or willful disregard of minor status; parental consent for under-13; minor consent (opt-in) for 13-17.
procedural_safeguards: DPAs required; duty of care; CT AG enforcement.
technical_standards_refs: No exclusive external technical standard mandated; influential template for CO SB 24-041.

title: SB 162 / La. R.S. 51:1738 et seq. – Secure Online Child Interaction and Age Limitation Act (SOCIAL Act)
measure_text_summary: Requires social media platforms to perform third-party age verification and obtain parental consent for users under 16 in Louisiana; administered by LA Dept. of Justice; effective Jul 1, 2025 after one-year delay.
status_detail: In Force – Challenged; effective Jul 1, 2025 (amended from Jul 1, 2024 by HB 577, signed Jun 18, 2024); NetChoice v. Murrill (M.D. La.) filed Mar 18, 2025 before law took effect; litigation ongoing.
last_court_action: NetChoice v. Murrill (M.D. La.) – filed Mar 18, 2025
legal_obligations: Social media platforms must require third-party age verification for all users; parental consent for under-16s to create accounts; targeted advertising ban.
scope_of_application: Social media platforms serving Louisiana users under 16; excludes email, video games, streaming, news, sports, entertainment without user-generated content.
verification_requirements: Third-party age verification for all users; parental consent for under-16s.
procedural_safeguards: HB 577 (2024) delayed effective date one year and added targeted advertising ban; LA DOJ administration.
technical_standards_refs: No exclusive external technical standard mandated.

title: HB 61 – Louisiana Interactive Computer Service Parental Consent Act
measure_text_summary: Broader than SB 162, requires parental consent before any user under 18 creates an account on an interactive computer service in Louisiana; effective Aug 1, 2024.
status_detail: In force effective Aug 1, 2024; broader scope than SB 162 (SOCIAL Act) covering any interactive computer service; distinct from LA HB 77 (adult content AV) and LA Act 440.
legal_obligations: Operators of any interactive computer service must obtain parental consent before under-18 user creates account.
scope_of_application: Operators of any 'interactive computer service' serving Louisiana users under 18; broader scope than SB 162.
verification_requirements: Parental consent required before under-18 user creates account; age verification implied.
procedural_safeguards: Civil enforcement; no lawsuit confirmed against this bill specifically.
technical_standards_refs: No exclusive external technical standard mandated.

title: SB S7694A / NY Gen. Bus. Law § 1500 et seq. – Stop Addictive Feeds Exploitation (SAFE) for Kids Act
measure_text_summary: Requires covered operators providing addictive feeds to New York users under 18 to obtain parental consent; pending AG rulemaking; expected operative approximately mid-2027.
status_detail: Pending – Awaiting Rules; signed by Gov. Hochul Jun 20, 2024; AG released proposed rules Sep 2025; public comment closed Dec 1, 2025; law takes effect 180 days after final rules issued (~mid-2027).
legal_obligations: Covered operators must obtain verifiable parental consent for minors to access addictive feeds; civil penalty up to $5,000 per violation.
scope_of_application: 'Covered operators' providing 'addictive feeds' as a significant part of their online service to New York users under 18.
verification_requirements: Age determination via 'commercially reasonable and technically feasible methods' (ID upload, phone/email cross-check, credit card); verifiable parental consent for minors.
procedural_safeguards: AG rulemaking required before operative; civil penalties; no private right of action.
technical_standards_refs: AG proposed rules specify implementation details; no single exclusive technical standard mandated.

title: SB S7695B / NY Gen. Bus. Law § 899-ee et seq. – New York Child Data Protection Act (CDPA)
measure_text_summary: Companion to the SAFE for Kids Act; prohibits sale and processing of personal data of New York users under 18 without appropriate consent; effective Jun 20, 2025.
status_detail: In force effective Jun 20, 2025; companion to SAFE for Kids Act; both signed Jun 20, 2024; prohibits sale and processing of minors' personal data without consent; includes device-flag provisions.
legal_obligations: Operators must not sell or process personal data of users under 18 without informed consent (13-17) or parental consent (under 13); must delete data within 30 days of learning user is a minor.
scope_of_application: Operators of websites, apps, connected devices, or online services that process personal data of New York users under 18.
verification_requirements: Informed consent for 13-17; parental consent for under-13; device-flag compliance.
procedural_safeguards: 30-day data deletion obligation upon learning user is a minor; AG enforcement.
technical_standards_refs: Device-flag provisions; no exclusive external technical standard mandated.

title: HB 603 / SB 571 / Md. Code Ann. §§ 14-4801 to 14-4813 – Maryland Age-Appropriate Design Code Act (Maryland Kids Code)
measure_text_summary: Second US AADC; requires for-profit companies offering online products 'reasonably likely to be accessed by children' to act in best interests of children, prohibit dark patterns, and conduct DPIAs; under active constitutional challenge.
status_detail: In Force – Challenged; effective Oct 1, 2024; DPIA compliance deadline Jul 1, 2026; NetChoice v. Brown (D. Md.) filed Feb 3, 2025; preliminary injunction motion pending as of Apr 2026.
last_court_action: NetChoice v. Brown (D. Md.) filed Feb 3, 2025; preliminary injunction motion pending
legal_obligations: For-profit companies must act in best interests of children; prohibit dark patterns, geolocation collection, addictive design; require DPIAs by Jul 1, 2026.
scope_of_application: For-profit companies with $25M+ revenue (or 50,000+ consumers) offering online products 'reasonably likely to be accessed by children' under 18.
verification_requirements: No mandatory age estimation; duty to act in 'best interests of children'; DPIAs required.
procedural_safeguards: DPIAs; civil penalties $2,500–$7,500 per child per violation; AG enforcement.
technical_standards_refs: No exclusive external technical standard mandated; modeled on UK Children's Code.

title: SB 24-041 – Colorado Privacy Act – Children's Privacy Amendments
measure_text_summary: Amends Colorado Privacy Act to add minors' protections; requires opt-in consent before targeted advertising, data sales, profiling, or addictive design for known or 'willfully disregarded' minors; effective Oct 1, 2025.
status_detail: In force Oct 1, 2025; signed May 31, 2024; no challenge filed as of Apr 2026; CO AG finalized implementing rules Dec 2024; cure period ends Dec 31, 2026.
legal_obligations: Entities must obtain opt-in consent before targeted advertising, data sales, profiling, or addictive design features for known minors; precise geolocation ban for known minors.
scope_of_application: Any entity conducting business in Colorado OR delivering products/services to CO residents that offers online services to consumers it knows or willfully disregards are minors under 18; no revenue/volume threshold.
verification_requirements: Actual knowledge or willful disregard of minor status; opt-in consent required; parental consent for under-13.
procedural_safeguards: CO AG implementing rules finalized Dec 2024; cure period ends Dec 31, 2026; ski area operator geolocation exemption.
technical_standards_refs: No exclusive external technical standard mandated; modeled on CT SB 3.

title: HB 24-1136 – Colorado Social Media Warning Label Law
measure_text_summary: Requires certain social media platforms to display a large pop-up warning to users under 18 each time they open the platform and every 30 minutes thereafter; stayed by court before Jan 1, 2026 effective date.
status_detail: Stayed; court stayed implementation Nov 2025 before Jan 1, 2026 effective date in NetChoice v. Weiser (D. Colo.); distinct from CO SB 24-041 (privacy/data).
last_court_action: NetChoice v. Weiser (D. Colo.) – court stayed implementation Nov 2025
legal_obligations: Platforms must display large pop-up warning to under-18 users each time they open the platform (first daily use) and every 30 minutes thereafter.
scope_of_application: Certain social media platforms used by Colorado users under 18.
verification_requirements: No age verification required per se; warning label approach.
procedural_safeguards: Court stay in place; enforcement halted pending litigation.
technical_standards_refs: No exclusive technical standard mandated.

title: HB 33 (Part 5) – Ohio Social Media Parental Notification Act
measure_text_summary: Inserted into biennial budget bill; required parental consent before under-16 user creates social media or online gaming account in Ohio. Permanently enjoined before taking effect.
status_detail: Permanently enjoined; TRO Jan 9, 2024; permanent injunction Apr 16, 2025 (NetChoice LLC v. Yost, 2025 WL 1137485, S.D. Ohio); Ohio appealed to 6th Circuit May 12, 2025; appeal pending.
last_court_action: NetChoice LLC v. Yost – permanent injunction Apr 16, 2025; Ohio appealing to 6th Circuit
legal_obligations: Platforms must obtain parental consent before under-16 user creates account or enters contract with platform.
scope_of_application: Online gaming and social media platforms likely to be used by Ohio users under 16.
verification_requirements: Parental consent for under-16 account creation.
procedural_safeguards: Civil enforcement; subsequently replaced/superseded by OH HB 96 and broader legislation.
technical_standards_refs: No exclusive technical standard mandated.

title: SF 2105 / HF 1434 – Minnesota Age Verification for Harmful Content (Proposed)
measure_text_summary: Proposed Minnesota law requiring age verification to confirm users are 18+ before accessing websites with material harmful to minors; includes anonymous AV option; not yet enacted as of Apr 2026.
status_detail: Pending – Proposed; referred to Senate Commerce Committee Mar 3, 2025; House version referred to Commerce, Finance and Policy Mar 2025; not yet enacted as of Apr 2026.
enforcement_authority: Minnesota Attorney General (proposed); private right of action also proposed
legal_obligations: Websites with material harmful to minors must verify users are 18+ before access; anonymous AV option required.
scope_of_application: Websites with material harmful to minors accessible to Minnesota residents.
verification_requirements: Age verification to confirm user is 18+; anonymous AV option required.
procedural_safeguards: MN AG enforcement and private right of action as proposed.
technical_standards_refs: No exclusive technical standard mandated.

title: S.B. 142 (2025) / Utah Code § 13-75-101 et seq. – Utah App Store Accountability Act
measure_text_summary: First-in-nation app store accountability law; requires app store providers to conduct commercially reasonable age verification at account creation and categorise users into four age tiers; parental consent required for minor downloads and purchases.
status_detail: In force; signed Mar 26, 2025; effective May 7, 2025; compliance deadline May 6, 2026; private right of action for parents effective Dec 31, 2026; Utah Division of Consumer Protection to promulgate rules on verification methods by May 6, 2026.
enforcement_authority: Utah Division of Consumer Protection; Utah Attorney General; private right of action for parents (eff. Dec 31, 2026)
legal_obligations: App store providers must conduct commercially reasonable age verification at account creation; link minor accounts to verified parent accounts; obtain verifiable parental consent before downloads, purchases, and in-app purchases.
scope_of_application: App store providers operating in Utah; developers of apps made available through those stores.
verification_requirements: Commercially reasonable age verification categorising users as: Child (<13), Younger Teen (13–15), Older Teen (16–17), Adult (18+); minor account linked to verified parent account.
procedural_safeguards: Utah Division of Consumer Protection rulemaking by May 6, 2026; private right of action for parents eff. Dec 31, 2026; AG enforcement.
technical_standards_refs: No exclusive external technical standard mandated; Utah Division of Consumer Protection to issue rules on verification methods.

title: S.B. 2420 (89th Legislature, 2025) – Texas App Store Accountability Act
measure_text_summary: Modelled on Utah SB 142; required commercially reasonable age category verification at account creation with parental consent for minor downloads. Enjoined Dec 23, 2025 before Jan 1, 2026 effective date on First Amendment grounds.
status_detail: Enjoined; preliminary injunctions granted Dec 23, 2025 (SEAT v. Paxton; CCIA & NetChoice v. Paxton, W.D. Tex.) on First Amendment grounds — court found AV and parental consent requirements likely fail strict scrutiny; on appeal.
last_court_action: SEAT v. Paxton; CCIA & NetChoice v. Paxton (W.D. Tex.) – preliminary injunctions granted Dec 23, 2025
enforcement_authority: Texas Attorney General (deceptive trade practices enforcement)
legal_obligations: App store owners must conduct commercially reasonable age category verification at account creation; require parental consent for each minor download/purchase/in-app purchase; age ratings required for all apps.
scope_of_application: App store owners operating in Texas; developers of apps distributed through those stores to Texas users.
verification_requirements: Commercially reasonable age category verification (4 tiers: Child <13, Younger Teen 13–15, Older Teen 16–17, Adult 18+); parental consent for each minor download/purchase.
procedural_safeguards: Violations treated as deceptive trade practices; no statutory safe harbor for app stores.
technical_standards_refs: No exclusive external technical standard mandated.

title: H.B. 570 (2025 Regular Session) – Louisiana App Store Accountability Act
measure_text_summary: One of three states (Utah, Texas, Louisiana) to enact App Store Accountability Acts in 2025; requires commercially reasonable age verification at account creation and parental consent for minors; notable 12-month cap on re-verification requests; effective Jul 1, 2026.
status_detail: In force effective Jul 1, 2026; signed Jun 30, 2025; distinct from Utah/Texas: no developer safe harbor; 12-month cap on re-verification requests; Texas analogue enjoined Dec 23, 2025.
legal_obligations: App store providers must conduct commercially reasonable age verification at account creation; parental consent required for minors; no developer safe harbor.
scope_of_application: App store providers operating in Louisiana; developers of apps distributed through those stores.
verification_requirements: Commercially reasonable age verification at account creation; parental consent for minors; re-verification limited to once every 12 months.
procedural_safeguards: 12-month cap on re-verification requests; no developer safe harbor; AG enforcement.
technical_standards_refs: No exclusive external technical standard mandated.

title: Assembly Bill 105 / Senate Bill 130 (2025–26 Session) – Wisconsin Internet Age Verification Bill
measure_text_summary: Would have required commercially reasonable age verification for websites distributing material harmful to minors; vetoed by Gov. Tony Evers (D) Apr 3, 2026 citing privacy risks, lack of data-sharing restrictions, and security vulnerabilities.
status_detail: Failed — Vetoed Apr 3, 2026 by Gov. Tony Evers (D); passed Assembly 69-22 and Senate by voice vote with bipartisan support; Evers cited privacy risks, lack of data-sharing restrictions, and security vulnerabilities; encouraged device-based AV as alternative.
legal_obligations: Would have required commercially reasonable age verification (government-issued ID, biometric facial scan, credit card, or combinations) before access to material harmful to minors.
scope_of_application: Business entities publishing/distributing material harmful to minors on websites where such material comprises a substantial portion of content.
verification_requirements: Commercially reasonable age verification: government-issued ID, biometric facial scan, credit card, or combinations.
procedural_safeguards: Earlier draft included VPN ban (later removed); governor encouraged device-based AV alternative.
technical_standards_refs: No exclusive technical standard mandated.

title: S.B. 69 / Act 63 of 2025 / 9 V.S.A. § 2449a et seq. – Vermont Age-Appropriate Design Code Act
measure_text_summary: Fourth state AADC; requires covered businesses operating in Vermont to apply default high-level privacy settings for minors, prohibit dark patterns, restrict monitoring of minors' activity/location, and restrict recommending content without consent; operative Jan 1, 2027 pending AG rulemaking.
status_detail: In force / pending rulemaking; signed Jun 12, 2025; Jan 1, 2027 operative date pending AG rulemaking; governor acknowledged potential legal challenge at signing; no lawsuit filed as of Apr 2026.
enforcement_authority: Vermont Attorney General; private right of action also available
legal_obligations: Covered businesses must apply highest-level default privacy settings; prohibit dark patterns; restrict use of minor's data to select/recommend/prioritize content without consent; restrict monitoring of minor's activity/location without conspicuous signal.
scope_of_application: Covered businesses that conduct business in Vermont, generate majority of annual revenue from online services, collect VT consumers' personal data, and offer digital products/services 'reasonably likely to be accessed' by minors under 18.
verification_requirements: Age assurance methods to be specified by Vermont AG rulemaking (due by Jan 1, 2027); no mandatory AV gate.
procedural_safeguards: AG rulemaking due Jan 1, 2027; private right of action; AG enforcement.
technical_standards_refs: No exclusive external technical standard mandated; AG rulemaking to define age estimation methods.

title: L.B. 504 (109th Legislature, 2025) – Nebraska Age-Appropriate Online Design Code Act
measure_text_summary: Third state AADC (after CA 2022, MD 2024); narrower than CA/MD due to unusually high revenue threshold (50% from data sales); prohibits dark patterns, targeted advertising, and addictive design for known minors; AG enforcement from Jul 1, 2026.
status_detail: In force; signed May 30, 2025; effective Jan 1, 2026; AG enforcement from Jul 1, 2026; no challenge filed as of Apr 2026; part of Gov. Pillen's four-bill child-protection package.
legal_obligations: Covered services must reasonably estimate ages using available signals; apply default high-protection settings for known minors; prohibit dark patterns, targeted advertising, and addictive design features for known minors.
scope_of_application: Online services with annual gross revenue >$25M; annually process data of ≥50,000 consumers, households, or devices; and derive ≥50% of annual revenue from sale or sharing of consumers' personal data.
verification_requirements: No mandatory age gate; safe harbor for controllers conducting age verification or age-gating; duty of reasonable care for known-minor users.
procedural_safeguards: Violations = deceptive trade practice under NE Uniform Deceptive Trade Practices Act ($50K/violation max); AG enforcement from Jul 1, 2026.
technical_standards_refs: No exclusive external technical standard mandated.

title: S.B. 297 (69th Montana Legislature, 2025) – Montana CDPA Minor Protections Amendment
measure_text_summary: Amends Montana Consumer Data Privacy Act to add duty of care for known minors, prohibit targeted advertising and data sale of known minors, and ban design features that significantly increase minor use of social media.
status_detail: In force effective Oct 1, 2025; signed May 8, 2025; no challenge filed; adds duty of care aligning with CO and CT models; removes 60-day cure period; adds dark-patterns prohibition for consent mechanisms.
legal_obligations: Controllers must comply with duty of reasonable care for known minors; prohibit targeted advertising and data sale of known minors; ban design features that significantly increase minor use of social media; data protection assessments for heightened minor-harm risk.
scope_of_application: Any entity conducting business in Montana OR delivering commercial products/services intentionally targeted to Montana residents; no applicability thresholds for minor provisions.
verification_requirements: No mandatory age gate; safe harbor for controllers implementing commercially reasonable age estimation; duty of care standard for actual knowledge or wilful disregard of minor status.
procedural_safeguards: Removes 60-day cure period; adds dark-patterns prohibition for consent mechanisms; data protection assessments required.
technical_standards_refs: No exclusive external technical standard mandated.

title: S.B. 611 / Act 900 of 2025 – Arkansas Social Media Safety Act 2025 Amendment
measure_text_summary: Enacted days after AR-SB396 was permanently enjoined; attempts to cure constitutional defects with narrower social media definition and lower age threshold (under 16); most provisions likely still subject to the permanent injunction against Act 689.
status_detail: Partially Enjoined; signed Apr 21, 2025; permanent injunction against Act 689 (NetChoice LLC v. Griffin, W.D. Ark. Mar 31, 2025) extends to most provisions of Act 900; phased effective dates Aug 3, 2025 and Apr 21, 2026; curfew/notification provisions (eff. Apr 21, 2026) may not be covered by existing injunction; Arkansas appealing.
last_court_action: NetChoice LLC v. Griffin, 2025 WL 978607 (W.D. Ark. Mar 31, 2025) – permanent injunction extends to most Act 900 provisions
legal_obligations: Age verification for users; parental consent required for minors under 16; technology to prevent circumvention of age verification; curfew restrictions (no notifications 10pm–6am CST without parental modification, eff. Apr 21, 2026).
scope_of_application: Social media platforms as redefined in Act 900 (broader definition); users under 16.
verification_requirements: Age verification for users; parental consent required for minors under 16.
procedural_safeguards: Curfew and notification provisions (eff. Apr 21, 2026) may be outside scope of existing injunction; Arkansas appealing 6th Circuit.
technical_standards_refs: No exclusive external technical standard mandated.

title: H.B. 2008 (2025 Regular Session) – Oregon Data Privacy Act Children's Data Amendments
measure_text_summary: Amends Oregon Consumer Privacy Act to add a first-in-nation ban on sale of personal data of minors under 16 for targeted advertising; also bans sale of precise geolocation data; duty of reasonable care for known-minor users.
status_detail: In force effective Jan 1, 2026; signed May–Jun 2025; first-in-nation ban on SALE of personal data of minors under 16 for targeted advertising regardless of consent; EPIC praised as 'one of the best children's privacy laws in the country'.
legal_obligations: Controllers may not process minor data for targeted advertising, profiling, or sale; ban on sale of personal data of minors under 16; ban on sale of precise geolocation data of all Oregonians.
scope_of_application: Controllers subject to Oregon Consumer Privacy Act (ORS 646A.570) with actual knowledge or wilful disregard that a consumer is under 16.
verification_requirements: No mandatory age gate; safe harbor for controllers conducting age verification or age-gating; duty of reasonable care for known-minor users.
procedural_safeguards: COPPA-aligned provisions for under-13; AG enforcement.
technical_standards_refs: No exclusive external technical standard mandated.

title: HB 18, 88th Tex. Leg. (2023) / Tex. Bus. & Com. Code Ch. 509 – Securing Children Online through Parental Empowerment (SCOPE) Act
measure_text_summary: First Texas social media children's safety law; passed unanimously in Senate; imposes parental empowerment and safety-by-design obligations on social media platforms; age verification requirements enjoined alongside content filtering as of Feb 2025.
status_detail: Partially Enjoined; effective Sep 1, 2024; prelim. injunction (content monitoring/filtering) Aug 30, 2024 (Judge Pitman, W.D. Tex.); broader prelim. injunction (content monitoring, filtering, advertising, and AV requirements) Feb 7, 2025 (FIRE v. Paxton, N.D. Tex.); both on appeal to 5th Cir.
last_court_action: FIRE v. Paxton (N.D. Tex.) – broader preliminary injunction Feb 7, 2025; both cases on appeal to 5th Cir.
legal_obligations: Digital service providers must implement age registration, parental monitoring tools, minor data protections, and (for platforms distributing >1/3 harmful content) commercially reasonable AV.
scope_of_application: Digital service providers operating social platforms in Texas that connect users for social interaction, allow public/semi-public profiles, and allow user-generated content; small business exemption.
verification_requirements: Age registration at account creation; commercially reasonable AV required for platforms distributing >1/3 harmful/obscene content; parental verification for consent actions on behalf of minors.
procedural_safeguards: TX AG also sued social media platforms for SCOPE Act violations (Oct 2024); ongoing 5th Circuit appeal.
technical_standards_refs: No exclusive external technical standard mandated.

title: HB 1891 / SB 2097 – Tennessee Protecting Children from Social Media Act
measure_text_summary: Requires social media companies to perform third-party age verification of all users (new and existing accounts within 14 days) and obtain parental consent for under-18s; effective Jan 1, 2025; no preliminary injunction obtained by NetChoice as of Apr 2026.
status_detail: In Force – Challenged; effective Jan 1, 2025; NetChoice v. Skrmetti (M.D. Tenn.) filed Oct 3, 2024; no preliminary injunction as of Apr 2026; case management delayed pending U.S. v. Skrmetti SCOTUS ruling.
last_court_action: NetChoice v. Skrmetti (M.D. Tenn., Judge William L. Campbell Jr.) filed Oct 3, 2024; no preliminary injunction obtained as of Apr 2026
legal_obligations: Social media companies must perform third-party age verification of all users (new and existing accounts within 14 days); obtain parental consent for under-18s; provide parental supervision tools (daily time limits, privacy settings); not retain identifying data post-verification.
scope_of_application: Social media companies with Tennessee users; exemptions for email, online shopping, career development, cloud storage, ISPs, and peer-to-peer payment platforms.
verification_requirements: Third-party age verification of all users including retroactive re-verification of existing accounts within 14 days.
procedural_safeguards: No retention of identifying data post-verification; parental supervision tools; civil enforcement.
technical_standards_refs: No exclusive external technical standard mandated.

title: SB 854 (2025) / Va. Code Ann. § 59.1-577.1 – Virginia VCDPA Amendment – Social Media Minor Use Restrictions
measure_text_summary: Amends Virginia Consumer Data Protection Act to require commercially reasonable age determination for social media platform users; imposes one-hour daily limit on identified minors per service; effective Jan 1, 2026.
status_detail: In force effective Jan 1, 2026; signed May 2, 2025; passed Virginia General Assembly unanimously; no challenge confirmed as of Apr 2026.
legal_obligations: Social media platform operators must apply commercially reasonable age determination; impose one-hour daily limit on identified minors per service; obtain verifiable parental consent to modify daily limit; prohibit use of collected age data for any other purpose.
scope_of_application: Social media platform operators with Virginia users that connect users for social interaction, allow profile creation, and allow user-generated content.
verification_requirements: Commercially reasonable age determination methods (e.g., neutral age screen) to determine if user is under 16.
procedural_safeguards: One-hour daily limit per service; verifiable parental consent to modify limit; AG enforcement; civil penalties up to $7,500 per violation; no private right of action.
technical_standards_refs: No exclusive external technical standard mandated.

title: S. 2714 / H.R. 7218 (119th Congress) – Children Harmed by AI Technology Act (CHAT Act)
measure_text_summary: Proposed federal regulation of companion AI chatbots; requires commercially available age verification and parental consent before minor access; mandates blocking sexually explicit content to identified minors; FTC enforcement.
status_detail: Pending – Proposed/Introduced; S. 2714 introduced Sep 4, 2025 by Sen. Jon Husted (R-OH); H.R. 7218 introduced Jan 22, 2026; referred to Senate Commerce, Science, and Transportation Committee; not enacted as of Apr 2026.
legal_obligations: Covered entities operating companion AI chatbots must implement age verification at account creation; parental account linkage and verifiable parental consent before minor access; block sexually explicit content to minors; monitor for suicidal ideation; 60-minute pop-up human-disclosure requirement.
scope_of_application: Covered entities operating 'companion AI chatbots' — AI systems for emotional/social/therapeutic simulation; does NOT cover general-purpose LLMs or AI models.
verification_requirements: Commercially available age verification at account creation for all users; parental account linkage and verifiable parental consent before minor access.
procedural_safeguards: FTC has 180-day guidance period post-enactment; good-faith compliance defence available.
technical_standards_refs: No single mandatory external technical standard identified in proposal text.

title: S. 737 / H.R. 1623 (119th Congress) – Shielding Children's Retinas from Egregious Exposure on the Net Act (SCREEN Act)
measure_text_summary: Federal analogue to state adult-content AV laws; requires covered platforms hosting content harmful to minors to implement technology verification to ensure users are not minors; FTC enforcement with mandatory regular audits.
status_detail: Pending – Proposed/Introduced; S. 737 reintroduced by Sen. Mike Lee (R-UT); H.R. 1623 by Rep. Mary Miller (R-IL); referred to Senate Commerce Committee and House Energy and Commerce Committee; not enacted as of Apr 2026.
enforcement_authority: Federal Trade Commission (proposed); mandatory regular audits
legal_obligations: Covered platforms must implement technology verification measures to ensure users are not minors; must apply to IP addresses including known VPN IPs; publish verification process; data security requirements for collected verification data.
scope_of_application: Interactive computer services for which creating/hosting content harmful to minors is in the regular course of business for profit; primarily targets adult content platforms.
verification_requirements: Technology verification measures to ensure users are not minors; must apply to known VPN IPs; FTC issues guidance and conducts regular audits.
procedural_safeguards: FTC guidance and mandatory regular audits; data security requirements for collected verification data.
technical_standards_refs: No single mandatory external technical standard identified in proposal text.

title: H.R. 7757 (119th Congress) – Kids Internet and Digital Safety Act (KIDS Act)
measure_text_summary: Broader children's online safety omnibus incorporating Safe Social Media Act, AWARE Act (AI warnings), and SAFEBOTs Act (AI chatbot safety); passed House Energy & Commerce Committee Mar 5, 2026; requires technology verification to prevent minor access to harmful content.
status_detail: Pending – Passed House Energy & Commerce Committee Mar 5, 2026; introduced Mar 3, 2026 by Rep. Brett Guthrie (R-KY); FTC enforcement; not enacted as of Apr 2026.
legal_obligations: Technology verification measures on covered platforms to prevent minor access to harmful content; parental control enhancements; prohibition on ephemeral messaging for minors; targeted market research ban on children; GAO report mandate on effectiveness of AV technologies.
scope_of_application: Covered platforms where substantial portion (>1/3) of content is harmful to minors; social media platforms, online gaming, messaging apps; minors defined as under 17.
verification_requirements: Technology verification measures to prevent minor access to harmful content; incorporates age verification study requirements.
procedural_safeguards: GAO report mandate on effectiveness of AV technologies; FTC enforcement.
technical_standards_refs: No single mandatory external technical standard identified in proposal text; GAO study mandated.
